title: Project Skeleton and Configuration
purpose: Create the initial package or notebook-friendly project structure so the work is organized around reusable functions instead of one long notebook script.
scope: Create folders for source code, notebooks, data, and saved tables
Choose one city-labeling convention and document it
Define default settings such as fixed start city and brute-force city cap
outputs: Short README or notes describing defaults
acceptance_criteria: Project has a clear structure for code, notebooks, and data
Default start city is defined
Brute-force safety cap is defined
A future experiment can import shared code instead of copying it

title: Asymmetric Distance Table Data Model
purpose: Define how cities and asymmetric distance tables are represented in memory and, later, on disk.
scope: Choose table representation such as list of lists or NumPy array
Represent invalid diagonal entries consistently
Document the meaning of rows, columns, and directed distances
outputs: Validation rules for shape and values
acceptance_criteria: Table representation is documented
Asymmetry is allowed by design
Diagonal handling is clearly defined
The same table format can be used by all algorithms

title: Distance Table Generator
purpose: Build the function that creates a new asymmetric distance table for a chosen number of cities.
scope: Generate random positive distances for all off-diagonal entries
Support optional seed for reproducibility
Honor the chosen city-labeling convention
acceptance_criteria: Generated table is n x n
Diagonal is invalid or ignored as specified
All off-diagonal entries are positive
Table may be asymmetric
Using the same seed reproduces the same table

title: Saved Table Loader and Save/Use Flag
purpose: Support the workflow where experiments can either generate a new table or use a saved table.
scope: Define file format for saved tables
Create load function
Define and document the flag that chooses generation versus loading
outputs: Documented behavior for table source selection
acceptance_criteria: A saved table can be loaded and reused
The system clearly chooses between new-table mode and saved-table mode
Loaded tables are validated before use

title: Path Validation Function
purpose: Create a shared validator that checks whether a proposed Hamiltonian path is legal under the project rules.
scope: Confirm path begins at the fixed start city
Confirm every city appears exactly once
Reject missing cities, duplicates, or malformed paths
acceptance_criteria: Valid paths are accepted
Paths with repeated cities are rejected
Paths with the wrong start city are rejected
Paths with the wrong number of cities are rejected

title: Path Scoring Function
purpose: Create the shared function that computes the total cost of a valid Hamiltonian path using the asymmetric distance table.
scope: Sum directed edge costs along the path
Use the same scoring logic for all algorithms
Handle invalid paths cleanly
acceptance_criteria: Scoring follows path order exactly
Directed edges use row-to-column cost correctly
Known sample paths produce correct totals
Invalid paths are rejected or handled consistently

title: Guess-and-Check Algorithm
purpose: Implement the random-search baseline that generates valid Hamiltonian paths from the fixed start city and keeps the best one found.
scope: Generate valid random paths
Run for a configurable number of trials
Return best path and best cost found
outputs: Optional metadata such as trials completed
acceptance_criteria: Algorithm only evaluates valid paths
Best-so-far result updates correctly
Result starts at the fixed start city
Repeated runs with the same seed reproduce the same sequence if designed that way

title: Greedy Algorithm
purpose: Implement the nearest-unvisited-city heuristic from the fixed start city.
scope: At each step choose the cheapest outgoing edge to an unvisited city
Build a complete Hamiltonian path
Return path and cost
acceptance_criteria: Algorithm starts at the fixed start city
Each next choice is the cheapest legal unvisited move
Output is a valid Hamiltonian path
Reported cost matches the shared scoring function

title: Brute-Force Algorithm with Safety Cap
purpose: Implement the exhaustive search that checks every valid Hamiltonian path beginning at the fixed start city, but only for small city counts.
scope: Generate all legal permutations from the fixed start city
Evaluate every candidate path
Return optimal path and optimal cost
Skip execution when city count exceeds the safety cap
outputs: Skipped status and reason when not run
acceptance_criteria: Algorithm checks every valid candidate consistent with the fixed start city
Best path returned is truly minimal for tested small cases
The algorithm is skipped cleanly when n exceeds the cap
Skip reason is reported clearly

title: Annealing Algorithm
purpose: Implement the heuristic improvement algorithm that begins with a valid path from the fixed start city and explores neighboring paths while occasionally accepting worse moves.
scope: Create initial valid path
Create neighboring paths while preserving fixed start city
Track current path and best-ever path
Support basic annealing parameters
outputs: Optional metadata such as iterations completed
acceptance_criteria: Neighbor generation preserves a valid Hamiltonian path structure
Fixed start city remains fixed
Best-ever path is tracked correctly
Returned result is valid and score-consistent

title: Unified Result Schema
purpose: Define one common result format for all algorithms so the comparison layer can treat them consistently.
scope: Standardize fields such as algorithm name, ran or skipped status, path, cost, and notes
Support missing values for skipped algorithms
Make result objects easy to turn into a summary table later
acceptance_criteria: All algorithm results can be represented in the same schema
Skipped algorithms have a clean representation
Comparison logic does not need special-case chaos

title: Comparison Table Builder
purpose: Build the code that assembles normalized results into a clean human-readable comparison table.
scope: Display algorithm name, ran status, best path, path cost, guaranteed-optimal note, and notes
Sort or present results consistently
Keep the output classroom-friendly
outputs: Comparison table or equivalent summary object
acceptance_criteria: All four algorithms appear in the same comparison output
Skipped brute-force runs are shown clearly
Best paths and costs are readable and consistent

title: Main Experiment Function
purpose: Wrap the whole workflow into one reusable function that can later be called with different city counts and settings.
scope: Accept city count, fixed start city, and table-source flag
Prepare the table by generating or loading it
Run all four algorithms under shared rules
Return the table used and the comparison-ready results
acceptance_criteria: One function call can run the complete experiment
The function works for different city counts within project limits
The same function supports both generated and saved tables
The return value is structured and reusable

title: Notebook Demonstration
purpose: Create the first clean notebook that demonstrates the full MVP on one city count and one table.
scope: Explain the objective and vocabulary
Show the chosen or generated table
Run the main experiment function
Display the comparison output
acceptance_criteria: Notebook runs from top to bottom
Notebook shows one complete experiment clearly
Notebook is readable enough for classroom discussion

title: Correctness Test Cases
purpose: Create small, known examples that verify table handling, path validation, scoring, and algorithm behavior on manageable inputs.
scope: Build tiny hand-checkable tables
Write expected valid paths and costs
Verify brute force returns the known optimum on small examples
acceptance_criteria: Path validation passes and fails where expected
Path scoring matches hand calculations
Brute force confirms known optima on tiny cases
At least one sanity-check example exists for each algorithm
